insight: In Prismata, clicking a unit prevents it from blocking that turn, creating a direct tradeoff between using a unit's click ability and its defensive value. This means clicking an economy unit is never 'free' — it costs you 1 HP of defense for that turn.
author: p0lari
context: Discussion of a proposed 'Architect' unit design where p0lari explained the defensive cost of clicking units versus holding them as blockers.

insight: Energy (blue resource) is generally useless in the late game in most sets, meaning units that produce energy have diminishing value as games progress. Engineers are primarily bought in the late game for their HP as defenders, not for their energy production.
author: p0lari
context: Analysis of the proposed Architect unit's value proposition, where p0lari argued that lategame energy production is rarely useful.

insight: Early-game Engineers that don't provide immediate block value are inefficient because they occupy resources that could be Drones. A Drone is superior early because it both produces gold and can block, meaning it recovers its cost quickly, whereas an overpriced Engineer provides zero block value in the early game.
author: steel0229e
context: Steel evaluated the proposed Architect unit and explained the general principle of why overcosted engineers are weak early.

insight: Wild Drone's supply was reduced to 4 (from 10) as a balance measure because the ramp openings enabled by mass Wild Drone purchases were considered too strong. The developer (apooche) noted it could return to 10 supply if the unit received an appropriate nerf to its stats.
author: apooche
context: Discussion of Wild Drone's supply cap and its history, with apooche providing context on why it was limited.

insight: Plasmafier synergizes with drone-sacrifice themes and can help catch up in attack, making it relevant in strategies that involve sacrificing drones for burst economy or aggression. Its combination of drone saccing and attack generation makes it notably stronger than a simple drone-doubling effect.
author: mrguy888
context: mrguy888 analyzed a hypothetical drone-doubling unit design and concluded Plasmafier already fills a related niche more elegantly.

insight: Alternative high-HP drone variants in Prismata (e.g. Trinity, Ossified Drone, Thorium Dynamo) generally require sacrificing drones as part of their cost, while the few that do not (Wild Drone, Ebb Turbine) are intentionally not meant to be mass-produced. This design pattern keeps unit text simple and prevents complicated board states.
author: supergreyman
context: Discussion of a proposed frontline unit that buffs drone HP, where Greyman noted the design pattern of high-HP drone alternatives.

insight: Frontline units with the Fragile keyword function like backline Fragile units in terms of partial damage application — opponents can allocate exact damage to kill them without overkill, similar to how you can partially damage a Gauss Cannon.
author: mrguy888
context: Discussion of a proposed frontline fragile defender unit where the interaction between frontline and fragile keywords was clarified.

insight: Units that only cost a single tech resource (e.g., 1B or 1R) and provide minimal effect are problematic as designs because they become annoying board-space clutter without contributing meaningfully to gameplay.
author: apooche
context: apooche commented on a proposed low-cost tech unit design during a unit design discussion thread.

insight: In high-inflation game states, buying a 3-cost Husk is strictly better than holding a Drone and buying an Engineer, unless the Engineer's energy production has a specific use. The only exception is certain Deadeye Operative edge cases.
author: punf
context: Discussion of a proposed 3-cost Husk variant where punf noted the general strategic dominance of cheap defenders over the hold-drone-buy-engi line in high inflation.
